questions.stem: Spark can run on top of several cluster schedulers. Which of the following is a valid set of cluster managers Spark can use?

options.label: A
options.text: Hadoop YARN, Mesos, and the Standalone Spark Scheduler
options.rationale: Spark can use Hadoop YARN (the standard Hadoop scheduler), a Mesos cluster, or its own simple Standalone Spark Scheduler that ships with Spark.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark SQL, MLlib, and GraphX
options.rationale: These are higher-level Spark libraries that run on top of Spark Core, not cluster schedulers. The valid schedulers are YARN, Mesos, and the Standalone Spark Scheduler.

options.label: C
options.text: HDFS, Amazon S3, and the local file system
options.rationale: These are underlying storage systems, not cluster managers. Spark's scheduler options are YARN, Mesos, and the Standalone Spark Scheduler.

options.label: D
options.text: Driver, Executor, and Worker schedulers
options.rationale: Driver, executor, and worker are runtime roles in a Spark application, not external cluster managers. The cluster managers are YARN, Mesos, and the Standalone Spark Scheduler.

questions.stem: In a Spark application, what is the role of the driver program, and how does it connect to the cluster?

options.label: A
options.text: It contains the main method and defines the workflow, accessing the cluster through a SparkContext object
options.rationale: The driver program contains the main method, defines the workflow, and accesses Spark through the SparkContext, which is its connection to the cluster.

options.label: B
options.text: It is one of the worker processes that runs operations on RDD partitions
options.rationale: Worker-side processes that run operations on partitions are the executors, not the driver. The driver holds the main method and coordinates via SparkContext.

options.label: C
options.text: It is the cluster manager that allocates resources to applications
options.rationale: Resource allocation is handled by a cluster manager such as YARN, Mesos, or the Standalone scheduler. The driver runs the main method and connects through SparkContext.

options.label: D
options.text: It stores all RDD partitions in its own process memory
options.rationale: RDD partitions live in the executors across cluster nodes, not in the driver's process. The driver defines the workflow and connects to the cluster via SparkContext.

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly orders these milestones in the history of big data from earliest to latest?

options.label: A
options.text: Term 'Big Data' emerges -> Google introduces MapReduce -> Hadoop is unveiled -> NoSQL and in-memory computing rise
options.rationale: The term emerged in the 1990s, MapReduce came in 2004, Hadoop was unveiled in 2006, and NoSQL/in-memory/cloud characterize the 2010-to-now era, giving this exact order.

options.label: B
options.text: Google introduces MapReduce -> Term 'Big Data' emerges -> Hadoop is unveiled -> NoSQL and in-memory computing rise
options.rationale: This places MapReduce (2004) before the term emerging (1990s), which is backwards; the term came first in the 1990s.

options.label: C
options.text: Hadoop is unveiled -> Google introduces MapReduce -> Term 'Big Data' emerges -> NoSQL and in-memory computing rise
options.rationale: This puts Hadoop (2006) before MapReduce (2004) and the 1990s term, reversing several real dates.

options.label: D
options.text: Term 'Big Data' emerges -> Hadoop is unveiled -> Google introduces MapReduce -> NoSQL and in-memory computing rise
options.rationale: This swaps Hadoop and MapReduce; MapReduce (2004) preceded Hadoop's unveiling (2006).

questions.stem: BASE is the NoSQL alternative to ACID for transaction rules. What does the acronym BASE stand for?

options.label: A
options.text: Balanced, Atomic, Secure, Encrypted
options.rationale: Wrong. These terms are invented and do not match BASE. BASE stands for Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency.

options.label: B
options.text: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency
options.rationale: Correct. BASE expands to Basically Available, Soft state, and Eventual consistency, the relaxed consistency model NoSQL systems favor over strict ACID.

options.label: C
options.text: Bulk Access, Synchronous Execution, Exactly-once
options.rationale: Wrong. These do not correspond to BASE. The acronym is Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency.

options.label: D
options.text: Basically Atomic, Strong consistency, Enforced isolation
options.rationale: Wrong. This mixes in ACID-like properties (strong consistency, isolation). BASE deliberately relaxes those into eventual consistency and soft state.

questions.stem: In comparing the data models of SQL and NoSQL systems, which pairing correctly matches each paradigm to its model?

options.label: A
options.text: SQL: collections, documents, and graphs; NoSQL: tables and rows
options.rationale: Wrong. This reverses the two. Collections/documents/graphs are the NoSQL models, while tables/rows are the relational (SQL) model.

options.label: B
options.text: SQL: relational tables and rows; NoSQL: collections, documents, or graphs
options.rationale: Correct. SQL systems use a relational model of tables and rows, while NoSQL systems use non-relational models such as collections, documents, or graphs.

options.label: C
options.text: SQL: nodes and edges; NoSQL: tables and rows
options.rationale: Wrong. Nodes and edges describe a graph NoSQL model, not SQL. SQL uses tables/rows and NoSQL uses non-relational structures.

options.label: D
options.text: Both SQL and NoSQL use a relational table-and-row model
options.rationale: Wrong. Only SQL is relational. NoSQL is explicitly non-relational, using collections, documents, or graphs.

questions.stem: A dataset is organized as folders like /sales/year=2026/month=05/. When a query filters WHERE year=2026 AND month=5, the engine scans only that folder and skips month=04 and month=06. What is this optimization called?

options.label: A
options.text: Partition pruning
options.rationale: Correct. Organizing files into folder hierarchies by frequently filtered columns lets the engine skip irrelevant partitions; reducing data scanned this way is partition pruning.

options.label: B
options.text: Schema-on-read
options.rationale: Schema-on-read refers to applying table structure at query time over stored files. It does not describe skipping folders based on filter values, which is partition pruning.

options.label: C
options.text: Columnar projection
options.rationale: Reading only required columns is a columnar storage benefit, not the act of skipping whole folders based on partition filters. The folder-skipping behavior is partition pruning.

options.label: D
options.text: Block replication
options.rationale: Block replication relates to copying data blocks across nodes (an HDFS concept) for durability, not to skipping irrelevant partition folders, which is partition pruning.

questions.stem: What is the role of executors on worker nodes in a Spark application?

options.label: A
options.text: They define the workflow and hold the application's main method
options.rationale: Defining the workflow and holding the main method is the driver's job. Executors are the processes that run the specified operations on RDD partitions.

options.label: B
options.text: They are processes that run the driver-specified operations on their RDD partition and have a cache
options.rationale: Worker nodes run the application via executors; each executor runs the operations specified in the driver on its partition of the RDD and has a cache.

options.label: C
options.text: They allocate cluster resources to competing applications
options.rationale: Resource allocation is the cluster manager's responsibility. Executors execute the actual RDD operations on their partitions.

options.label: D
options.text: They store the only authoritative copy of the program's local variables
options.rationale: Local variables live in the driver's process memory, not on executors. Executors hold RDD partitions and a cache and run operations on them.

questions.stem: Which characteristic best describes the 'non-relational' pillar in the core anatomy of NoSQL databases?

options.label: A
options.text: It works with self-contained aggregates or BLOBs and avoids complex referential-integrity joins
options.rationale: Correct. The non-relational pillar means data is handled as self-contained aggregates or BLOBs, avoiding complex referential-integrity joins and object-relational mapping.

options.label: B
options.text: It enforces foreign-key constraints across many normalized tables
options.rationale: Wrong. Foreign keys across normalized tables describe the relational model. Non-relational NoSQL specifically avoids these complex referential-integrity joins.

options.label: C
options.text: It mandates a fixed schema for every record in a domain
options.rationale: Wrong. A fixed mandatory schema is the relational, schema-on-write idea. Non-relational NoSQL favors self-contained aggregates without such rigidity.

options.label: D
options.text: It guarantees full ACID transactions across all aggregates
options.rationale: Wrong. Non-relational design typically relaxes ACID. Its defining trait is using self-contained aggregates/BLOBs rather than guaranteeing ACID across joins.

questions.stem: Within the 5 Vs framework, which characteristic captures the usefulness and business worth of data, where higher fidelity and accuracy tend to increase it?

options.label: A
options.text: Volume
options.rationale: Volume is about sheer data size, not the worth derived from data. The characteristic describing usefulness and business worth is Value.

options.label: B
options.text: Veracity
options.rationale: Veracity concerns trustworthiness and quality. While higher veracity raises value, the V that names usefulness and business worth itself is Value.

options.label: C
options.text: Value
options.rationale: Value is defined as the usefulness or business value of data, and higher fidelity and veracity lead to more value, exactly as stated.

options.label: D
options.text: Variety
options.rationale: Variety addresses the diversity of data formats and sources, not its usefulness. The characteristic for usefulness and business worth is Value.

questions.stem: In a Medallion architecture, which layer contains raw ingested data stored exactly as received from the source, such as original JSON logs?

options.label: A
options.text: Gold Layer (Curated Zone)
options.rationale: The Gold Layer holds business-ready aggregated data for reporting, not untouched raw source data. Raw, as-received data belongs to the Bronze Layer.

options.label: B
options.text: Bronze Layer (Raw Zone)
options.rationale: Correct. The Bronze Layer (Raw Zone) stores raw ingested data exactly as received from the source, such as original JSON logs or raw registration files.

options.label: C
options.text: Silver Layer (Cleaned Zone)
options.rationale: The Silver Layer contains cleaned, joined, and standardized data with invalid records removed, not raw data exactly as received. That raw stage is the Bronze Layer.

options.label: D
options.text: Platinum Layer (Archive Zone)
options.rationale: There is no Platinum Layer in the Medallion architecture, which consists of Bronze, Silver, and Gold. Raw as-received data is held in the Bronze Layer.

questions.stem: By default, HDFS splits large files into fixed-size blocks and automatically maintains a replication factor of three. What is the main purpose of storing three copies of each block?

options.label: A
options.text: To enable low-latency random reads of small records
options.rationale: HDFS is optimized for high-throughput batch access, not low-latency random reads, and replication does not provide that. Replication exists for fault tolerance and high availability.

options.label: B
options.text: To compress the data and reduce total storage usage
options.rationale: Replication increases storage usage rather than reducing it, and it is unrelated to compression. Its purpose is fault tolerance and high availability.

options.label: C
options.text: To provide fault tolerance and high availability if a node holding a copy fails
options.rationale: Replicating each block three times across DataNodes ensures the data remains available and the cluster stays fault tolerant even when a node holding one copy fails.

options.label: D
options.text: To allow each replica to hold a different schema of the data
options.rationale: Replicas are identical copies of the same block, and HDFS is schema-less, so per-replica schemas make no sense. Replication's purpose is fault tolerance and high availability.

questions.stem: Apache Spark is described as a fast, general-purpose engine for large-scale data processing. Which set of goals best characterizes what Spark aims to achieve in the big-data context?

options.label: A
options.text: Generality, low latency, fault tolerance, and simplicity
options.rationale: These are exactly the design goals attributed to Spark: handling diverse workloads (generality), achieving sub-second response (low latency), treating faults as the norm (fault tolerance), and offering a simple model that often follows from generality.

options.label: B
options.text: Strong consistency, ACID transactions, and relational normalization
options.rationale: These are goals of a transactional relational database, not Spark. Spark's stated goals are generality, low latency, fault tolerance, and simplicity, not transactional guarantees.

options.label: C
options.text: Disk-only durability, batch-only execution, and Java-only programming
options.rationale: These describe Hadoop MapReduce's constraints, not Spark's goals. Spark deliberately moves beyond disk-only storage and batch-only execution and supports multiple languages, aiming instead for generality, low latency, fault tolerance, and simplicity.

options.label: D
options.text: Maximizing disk I/O throughput across iterations
options.rationale: Spark was motivated precisely to reduce disk I/O by keeping data in memory, so maximizing disk I/O is the opposite of its intent. Its real goals are generality, low latency, fault tolerance, and simplicity.

questions.stem: In the classic master-slave MapReduce scheduling model, the JobTracker (master) and TaskTrackers (slaves) coordinate work. Which statement accurately describes how tasks are assigned?

options.label: A
options.text: The JobTracker pushes tasks to idle TaskTrackers as soon as it detects them
options.rationale: MapReduce scheduling is pull-based, not push-based; the JobTracker does not push tasks. TaskTrackers request (pull) tasks from the JobTracker.

options.label: B
options.text: TaskTrackers pull tasks by sending requests to the JobTracker, which assigns and monitors them
options.rationale: Scheduling is pull-based: TaskTrackers request tasks from the JobTracker, which accepts jobs, assigns Map/Reduce tasks, monitors status, and re-executes failed tasks.

options.label: C
options.text: TaskTrackers assign tasks directly to one another without the JobTracker
options.rationale: TaskTrackers do not assign work peer-to-peer; the JobTracker is the master that hands out tasks. TaskTrackers pull tasks from the JobTracker, not from each other.

options.label: D
options.text: The NameNode assigns Map and Reduce tasks to the DataNodes
options.rationale: The NameNode/DataNode roles belong to HDFS storage, not MapReduce scheduling. Task assignment is done by the JobTracker, with TaskTrackers pulling tasks.

questions.stem: Spark's basic idea is to keep more data in main memory. Roughly how much faster can sharing data between iterations via memory be, compared with going through disk?

options.label: A
options.text: About 10 to 100 times faster
options.rationale: For iterative jobs, sharing data between iterations through main memory rather than disk yields roughly a 10x to 100x speedup.

options.label: B
options.text: About 2 to 3 times faster
options.rationale: This understates the benefit. The in-memory sharing of data between iterations is reported as roughly 10 to 100 times faster than disk-based sharing, not merely 2 to 3 times.

options.label: C
options.text: Exactly 1000 times faster in all cases
options.rationale: There is no fixed 1000x guarantee; the speedup depends on the workload. The stated range is approximately 10 to 100 times faster than disk.

options.label: D
options.text: It is actually slower because memory access is more expensive
options.rationale: Memory access is far faster than disk, which is the whole point of Spark's design. The in-memory approach is roughly 10 to 100 times faster, not slower.

questions.stem: A team stores raw event files in a data lake without defining any table structure, and only later creates tables that read those files when an analyst runs a query. Which approach to schema management does this describe?

options.label: A
options.text: Schema-on-write, because the data must conform to a structure before it can be stored
options.rationale: Schema-on-write is the rigid model used by traditional SQL databases, where structure must be defined before insertion. The scenario explicitly stores files first with no structure, which is the opposite of schema-on-write, so the correct answer is schema-on-read.

options.label: B
options.text: Schema-on-read, because files are stored first and tables are defined later on top of them
options.rationale: Correct. A data lake stores files first and creates tables on top of those files only when needed, applying structure at query time. This is precisely the schema-on-read model described.

options.label: C
options.text: Normalized relational modeling, because the data is split into related tables at ingestion
options.rationale: Normalized relational modeling implies designing structured related tables up front, which is a schema-on-write concept. The scenario defines no structure at ingestion, so the file-first model of schema-on-read applies instead.

options.label: D
options.text: ACID-enforced ingestion, because every write is validated against a fixed schema
options.rationale: Validating every write against a fixed schema is schema-on-write enforcement, not the file-first lake approach. The scenario applies structure only at read time, making schema-on-read the right answer.

questions.stem: An architecture stores 1,000,000 files of 10 KB each, and engines like Spark or Presto run slowly because they must open, list, and schedule work for every file. Which problem does this illustrate, and what is the recommended fix?

options.label: A
options.text: The schema-drift problem; enforce a strict schema-on-write before ingestion
options.rationale: Schema drift concerns changing field definitions, not file count overhead. The scenario is about too many tiny files, solved by consolidating into larger files.

options.label: B
options.text: The small-files problem; consolidate into fewer, larger batched files such as 100 files of 100 MB
options.rationale: Correct. Millions of tiny files create metadata overhead that chokes engines; batching into fewer, larger files (e.g., 100 x 100 MB) minimizes overhead and maximizes throughput.

options.label: C
options.text: The data-swamp problem; deploy a data catalog to map the files
options.rationale: A data swamp arises from missing metadata that makes data unusable. Here the issue is engine overhead from too many tiny files, fixed by batching them into larger files.

options.label: D
options.text: The over-partitioning problem; remove all partition folders entirely
options.rationale: While over-partitioning can cause small files, the fix is not removing all partitions; it is consolidating into fewer larger files. Eliminating partitions would also remove partition pruning benefits.

questions.stem: In a big data architecture, which layer is responsible for importing and loading data from the source systems into the platform?

options.label: A
options.text: The data ingestion layer
options.rationale: Data ingestion is the layer that imports and loads data into the platform, using batch or real-time methods, exactly matching this responsibility.

options.label: B
options.text: The storage and processing layer
options.rationale: Storage and processing holds and transforms data after it arrives; the act of importing and loading data into the platform is the ingestion layer's job.

options.label: C
options.text: The analytics and servicing layer
options.rationale: Analytics and servicing produces insights and reports from already-stored data; bringing the data in is handled by the ingestion layer.

options.label: D
options.text: The governance, security, and monitoring layer
options.rationale: Governance, security, and monitoring is an overarching layer enforcing policies and protection; it does not perform the importing and loading, which is ingestion.

questions.stem: All of the following are characteristics typically associated with the traditional data approach EXCEPT:

options.label: A
options.text: Centralized relational databases with a predefined fixed schema
options.rationale: This IS a traditional characteristic: traditional systems use centralized relational databases with predefined, fixed schemas, so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: Built-in redundancy and replication across distributed nodes
options.rationale: This is the exception. Built-in redundancy and replication across distributed nodes is a big data characteristic; traditional systems are noted for limited fault tolerance.

options.label: C
options.text: Reliance on SQL with business intelligence and reporting
options.rationale: This IS traditional: traditional systems rely on SQL for BI and reporting, so it belongs to the traditional approach and is not the exception.

options.label: D
options.text: Batch processing of moderate, mostly structured data
options.rationale: This IS traditional: traditional systems handle moderate, structured data using batch processing, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: Within the core Hadoop architecture, which component serves as the resource management layer for the cluster?

options.label: A
options.text: HDFS
options.rationale: HDFS is the distributed storage layer, not the resource manager. Resource management is handled by YARN.

options.label: B
options.text: ZooKeeper
options.rationale: ZooKeeper provides distributed coordination services, not cluster-wide compute resource management. The resource management layer is YARN.

options.label: C
options.text: MapReduce
options.rationale: MapReduce is the computing engine/programming model for processing, not resource management. Cluster resources are managed by YARN.

options.label: D
options.text: YARN
options.rationale: YARN is the component dedicated to resource management within the Hadoop cluster.

questions.stem: Consider the analytical query SELECT AVG(Grade) FROM students run against a very large dataset. Why is a columnar format such as Parquet far more efficient than a row-based format such as CSV for this query?

options.label: A
options.text: Columnar storage automatically caches the entire table in memory before computing
options.rationale: The efficiency does not come from caching the whole table; it comes from reading only the needed columns. Caching the entire table would not bypass irrelevant data the way columnar reads do.

options.label: B
options.text: Columnar storage isolates and reads only the Grade column, bypassing irrelevant data
options.rationale: Correct. Columnar engines extract only the required columns, completely bypassing irrelevant fields, while row-based engines must read every field in every record, causing massive I/O overhead.

options.label: C
options.text: Row-based storage cannot compute aggregate functions at all
options.rationale: Row-based engines can compute aggregates; they are simply inefficient because they must read every field in every record. The advantage of columnar is reduced I/O, not row formats being incapable.

options.label: D
options.text: Columnar storage stores each row contiguously to speed up full-record reads
options.rationale: Storing each row contiguously describes row-based storage. Columnar storage groups data by column, which is exactly what lets it read only the Grade column and skip the rest.

questions.stem: An e-commerce platform needs (1) fast handling of shopping-cart and session data and (2) storage of a product catalog where items have widely varying attributes. Which pairing of NoSQL models best matches these two needs?

options.label: A
options.text: Key-value store for cart/session data; document store for the product catalog
options.rationale: Correct. Key-value stores excel at fast cart/session data, and document stores handle product catalogs with varied, flexible attributes, matching the two stated needs.

options.label: B
options.text: Graph database for cart/session data; column-family store for the product catalog
options.rationale: Wrong. Graph databases fit relationship-heavy data like social networks, and column-family stores fit analytics. Carts/sessions suit key-value, and varied catalogs suit document stores.

options.label: C
options.text: Column-family store for cart/session data; key-value store for the product catalog
options.rationale: Wrong. This reverses the fits. Carts/sessions are a key-value use case, while a richly structured, varying catalog is a document-store use case, not column-family.

options.label: D
options.text: Document store for cart/session data; graph database for the product catalog
options.rationale: Wrong. Fast carts/sessions are best served by a key-value store, and a varied catalog by a document store. Graph databases are aimed at relationship traversal, not catalogs.

questions.stem: In an HDFS cluster, which statement correctly describes the role of the NameNode?

options.label: A
options.text: It is the master that stores all metadata, including the mapping and location of files and blocks
options.rationale: The NameNode is the master and holds all metadata: access rights, file/block mapping and locations, timestamps, file counts, DataNode counts, and the transaction log location.

options.label: B
options.text: It is a slave that stores the actual data blocks on its local disk
options.rationale: Storing actual data blocks is the DataNode's job; DataNodes are the slaves/block servers. The NameNode is the master that stores only metadata, not the data blocks.

options.label: C
options.text: It sends periodic block reports to the DataNodes describing what it stores
options.rationale: Block reports flow from DataNodes up to the NameNode, not the reverse. The NameNode receives those reports and tracks metadata; it does not store blocks to report on.

options.label: D
options.text: It executes the user's Map and Reduce tasks on stored data
options.rationale: Executing Map/Reduce tasks is a processing responsibility (TaskTrackers), unrelated to HDFS metadata management. The NameNode's role is to maintain filesystem metadata.

questions.stem: Which NoSQL data model stores information as a massive hash table where each unique key points to a value, but cannot query the value's contents directly?

options.label: A
options.text: Document-oriented store
options.rationale: Wrong. A document store understands and can deeply query the value's internal structure. The model that only looks up by key and cannot query the value is the key-value store.

options.label: B
options.text: Key-value store
options.rationale: Correct. A key-value store is a giant hash table where each key maps to an opaque value (string, JSON, or BLOB), and it can only be queried by key, not by the value's contents.

options.label: C
options.text: Column-family store
options.rationale: Wrong. A column-family store organizes data by columns and excels at aggregations over a column. The hash-table, key-only-lookup model is the key-value store.

options.label: D
options.text: Graph database
options.rationale: Wrong. A graph database stores nodes and edges to traverse relationships. The simple key-to-value hash-table model that cannot query values is the key-value store.

questions.stem: A continuous data streaming pipeline needs a compact binary format that supports ongoing schema evolution, while still being row-oriented. Which file format best fits this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Avro
options.rationale: Correct. Avro is a row-based binary format well suited to data streaming and continuous schema evolution, though it is less ideal for deep analytical column queries.

options.label: B
options.text: Parquet
options.rationale: Parquet is a columnar binary format built for heavy analytics, not the row-based streaming-and-schema-evolution use case where Avro excels.

options.label: C
options.text: CSV
options.rationale: CSV is a human-readable text format with no schema and is highly inefficient at scale, lacking the binary compactness and schema-evolution support Avro provides for streaming.

options.label: D
options.text: ORC
options.rationale: ORC is a columnar binary format optimized for Hadoop/Hive analytics, not row-based streaming with schema evolution, which is Avro's strength.

questions.stem: Spark tracks lineage information for each RDD. What is the primary purpose of this lineage, and how is it represented?

options.label: A
options.text: To enforce relational constraints between RDDs, represented as a normalized schema
options.rationale: Lineage is not about relational constraints or schemas. It records how each RDD was built so lost data can be recomputed, and it is represented as a DAG, not a schema.

options.label: B
options.text: To efficiently recompute data lost to executor failures, represented as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
options.rationale: Spark records how each RDD was constructed so it can rebuild data lost when an executor fails; this lineage is represented as a DAG connecting input data and RDDs.

options.label: C
options.text: To compress RDDs in memory, represented as a hash index
options.rationale: Lineage is not a compression or indexing mechanism. It captures construction history to recompute lost partitions and is represented as a DAG, not a hash index.

options.label: D
options.text: To schedule cluster managers, represented as a priority queue
options.rationale: Lineage has nothing to do with scheduling cluster managers. Its purpose is fault recovery by recomputation, represented as a DAG of input data and RDDs.

questions.stem: A Medallion architecture produces a dashboard showing attendance rate and risk indicators, consumed primarily by Business Analysts and BI tools. Which layer provides this business-ready aggregated output?

options.label: A
options.text: Bronze Layer, because raw logs are aggregated directly into dashboards
options.rationale: The Bronze Layer holds raw, unprocessed source data used by Data Engineers, not aggregated dashboard-ready output. Business-ready aggregation occurs in the Gold Layer.

options.label: B
options.text: Silver Layer, because cleaned tables are the final reporting product
options.rationale: The Silver Layer provides cleaned and standardized data for Data Scientists, but it is not the final aggregated reporting product. Dashboards for BI tools come from the Gold Layer.

options.label: C
options.text: Gold Layer, because it holds business-ready aggregated data for reporting
options.rationale: Correct. The Gold Layer (Curated Zone) contains business-ready, aggregated data prepared for reporting and is consumed by Business Analysts and BI tools, such as an attendance-rate dashboard.

options.label: D
options.text: Raw Zone, because BI tools query source files directly
options.rationale: The Raw Zone (Bronze) stores untransformed source data; BI tools should not query raw files for finished dashboards. Business-ready reporting data lives in the Gold Layer.

questions.stem: Three major cloud providers are commonly identified for big data, and the Hadoop and Spark engines play distinct roles in the ecosystem. Which of the following statements is NOT accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Apache Spark is an in-memory engine supporting batch and streaming that is faster than MapReduce
options.rationale: This is an accurate description of Spark, so it is a true statement and not the inaccurate one being sought.

options.label: B
options.text: Apache Hadoop provides distributed storage via HDFS and processing via MapReduce on commodity hardware
options.rationale: This correctly describes Hadoop as fault-tolerant, horizontally scalable distributed storage and processing, so it is a true statement and not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: Oracle Cloud is one of the three major cloud providers identified for big data
options.rationale: This is the inaccurate statement: the three major cloud providers identified are Amazon (AWS), Microsoft (Azure), and Google (Google Cloud); Oracle Cloud is not among them.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud are the major cloud providers for big data
options.rationale: This correctly names the three major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud), so it is a true statement and not the inaccurate one.

questions.stem: During a network partition, a CP-edge database such as MongoDB or HBase behaves in which way?

options.label: A
options.text: It stays fully online and returns the most recent local data on every node
options.rationale: Wrong. Staying online and serving local data during a partition is AP behavior (e.g., Cassandra). CP systems instead block access to out-of-sync nodes to avoid stale reads.

options.label: B
options.text: It shuts down access to out-of-sync nodes to prevent stale reads
options.rationale: Correct. A CP-edge system favors consistency: during a partition it cuts off access to out-of-sync nodes so that no stale data is read.

options.label: C
options.text: It permanently deletes data on the minority side of the partition
options.rationale: Wrong. CP systems do not delete data on partition; they restrict access to out-of-sync nodes temporarily to preserve consistency.

options.label: D
options.text: It ignores the partition and continues accepting writes everywhere
options.rationale: Wrong. Accepting writes everywhere despite a partition risks inconsistency and is closer to AP behavior. CP systems restrict out-of-sync nodes to keep data consistent.

questions.stem: Which factor BEST explains why the Apache Hadoop framework emerged as an alternative to traditional approaches?

options.label: A
options.text: Relational databases were incapable of running any analytical query whatsoever
options.rationale: Relational databases run analytical queries well at modest scale; the issue was scaling and cost limits at huge volumes, not a total inability to query. The real driver was data exceeding their scale/cost/fault limits.

options.label: B
options.text: Enterprise storage hardware became free, removing all cost concerns
options.rationale: Enterprise storage was expensive, and that high cost was a motivating challenge, not the reverse. Hadoop's appeal was running on inexpensive commodity hardware, not free enterprise storage.

options.label: C
options.text: Government regulation mandated open-source distributed processing
options.rationale: No regulatory mandate drove Hadoop; technical and economic pressures from exponential data growth did. The actual driver was data outgrowing traditional systems' scalability and fault tolerance.

options.label: D
options.text: Exponential data growth exceeded the storage, scalability, and fault-tolerance limits of traditional relational databases and centralized processing
options.rationale: Hadoop emerged because data volumes grew exponentially beyond what relational databases and centralized processing could handle in terms of cost, scalability, fault tolerance, and throughput.

questions.stem: A data team builds a standardized student-course-attendance table by joining sources, removing invalid records, and normalizing fields. This output is intended mainly for Data Scientists. Which Medallion layer does it represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Bronze Layer (Raw Zone)
options.rationale: The Bronze Layer stores raw data exactly as received, not joined and standardized tables with invalid records removed. The described cleaning step defines the Silver Layer.

options.label: B
options.text: Gold Layer (Curated Zone)
options.rationale: The Gold Layer holds business-ready aggregated data for reporting consumed by Business Analysts, not the intermediate cleaned-and-joined table aimed at Data Scientists, which is the Silver Layer.

options.label: C
options.text: Silver Layer (Cleaned Zone)
options.rationale: Correct. The Silver Layer holds cleaned, joined, and standardized data with invalid records removed, such as a standardized student-course-attendance table, and is used by Data Scientists.

options.label: D
options.text: Ingestion Layer (Landing Zone)
options.rationale: An ingestion/landing zone corresponds to raw arrival (Bronze), not cleaned and standardized data. The cleaned, join-ready table is the Silver Layer.

questions.stem: Cassandra, CouchDB, and Dynamo are commonly classified on the AP edge of CAP. What behavior during a partition justifies this classification?

options.label: A
options.text: They stay online and return the most recent local data, accepting eventual consistency
options.rationale: Correct. AP-edge systems prioritize availability: during a partition they remain online and serve the most recent local data, accepting eventual consistency.

options.label: B
options.text: They block all reads and writes until every node re-synchronizes
options.rationale: Wrong. Blocking access until nodes re-sync is CP behavior favoring consistency. AP systems instead keep serving local data to stay available.

options.label: C
options.text: They guarantee every node returns identical data at the instant of the partition
options.rationale: Wrong. Guaranteeing identical data at every instant is strong consistency (CP/CA). AP systems accept temporary divergence and reconcile via eventual consistency.

options.label: D
options.text: They require a single master to acknowledge each write before responding
options.rationale: Wrong. A single-master acknowledgment pattern leans toward consistency, not availability under partition. AP systems serve local data without that coordination.

questions.stem: During MapReduce execution, which component is responsible for loading the data from an input split and converting it into the key-value pairs that the Mapper consumes?

options.label: A
options.text: The Partitioner
options.rationale: The partitioner decides which reduce task a given (K,V) pair goes to, after mapping. Converting a split's raw data into (K,V) pairs for the Mapper is the Record Reader's job.

options.label: B
options.text: The Output Format
options.rationale: Output Format defines how reducers' (K,V) pairs are written to output files, which is the end of the pipeline. Reading a split into (K,V) pairs for the Mapper is done by the Record Reader.

options.label: C
options.text: The Record Reader
options.rationale: The Record Reader loads data from an input split and converts it into key-value pairs that are then fed to the Mapper.

options.label: D
options.text: The Reducer
options.rationale: The Reducer aggregates the sorted intermediate keys after the map phase, well after data has been read. Turning a split into (K,V) pairs for the Mapper is the Record Reader's responsibility.

questions.stem: All of the following are among the six pillars of modern data storage EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: Files First, storing big data initially as files in scalable cloud buckets rather than rigid tables
options.rationale: This is a genuine pillar and not the exception: Files First captures storing data initially as files in scalable cloud buckets instead of rigid tables.

options.label: B
options.text: Smart Partitioning, organizing files logically so queries bypass irrelevant data
options.rationale: This is a genuine pillar and not the exception: Smart Partitioning organizes files so queries can skip irrelevant data through partition pruning.

options.label: C
options.text: Schema-on-Write Enforcement, requiring every record to match a fixed schema before storage
options.rationale: This is the exception because it is not a pillar. The pillars favor a files-first, schema-on-read model; schema-on-write enforcement describes the rigid traditional database approach, not modern lake storage.

options.label: D
options.text: Active Catalogs, a metadata layer that makes an unstructured lake visible, searchable, and usable
options.rationale: This is a genuine pillar and not the exception: Active Catalogs provide the metadata layer that keeps a lake searchable and usable rather than a swamp.

questions.stem: In the core Hadoop architecture, which component is responsible for distributed STORAGE of data across the cluster?

options.label: A
options.text: YARN
options.rationale: YARN provides resource management for the cluster, not data storage. The distributed storage layer is HDFS.

options.label: B
options.text: MapReduce
options.rationale: MapReduce is the computing engine and programming model for processing data, not the storage layer. Distributed storage is provided by HDFS.

options.label: C
options.text: HDFS
options.rationale: HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is the distributed filesystem responsible for storing data across the cluster.

options.label: D
options.text: Hive
options.rationale: Hive is an ecosystem tool that provides SQL-like access to HDFS, not the core storage layer itself. The core distributed storage component is HDFS.

questions.stem: A Spark program declares both ordinary program variables and RDDs. Which statement correctly distinguishes where each is stored?

options.label: A
options.text: Local variables are stored across cluster nodes, while RDDs live only in the driver's memory
options.rationale: This reverses the truth. Local variables reside in the driver's process memory, while RDDs are distributed across cluster nodes.

options.label: B
options.text: Both local variables and RDDs are allocated in the driver's process memory
options.rationale: Only local variables live in the driver's memory; RDDs are distributed 'variables' stored across the cluster's executors, not in the driver.

options.label: C
options.text: Local variables are allocated in the driver's process memory, while RDDs are distributed across cluster nodes
options.rationale: Local variables are standard program variables held in the driver's process memory, whereas RDDs are distributed 'variables' whose partitions are stored across the nodes of the cluster.

options.label: D
options.text: Both local variables and RDDs are split into partitions across executors
options.rationale: Only RDDs are partitioned across executors; ordinary local variables remain in the driver's process memory.

questions.stem: What distinguishes a document-oriented store from a key-value store in terms of how the database treats the stored value?

options.label: A
options.text: A document store treats every value as an opaque BLOB it cannot inspect
options.rationale: Wrong. Treating the value as an opaque, uninspectable blob describes a key-value store. A document store understands the document's internal structure and can query inside it.

options.label: B
options.text: A document store understands the document's internal structure, enabling deep querying of its fields
options.rationale: Correct. Unlike a key-value store, a document store understands the internal structure of documents (JSON, BSON, XML), allowing deep querying of nested fields.

options.label: C
options.text: A document store can only retrieve data by primary key, never by field
options.rationale: Wrong. Retrieving only by key is the key-value limitation. Document stores can query by fields inside the document because they understand its structure.

options.label: D
options.text: A document store forbids nested or embedded data within a record
options.rationale: Wrong. Document stores explicitly allow lists, nested/embedded data, and missing fields. Forbidding nesting contradicts their core flexibility.

questions.stem: A traditional SQL database requires that a table's columns and types be defined before any rows can be inserted. What is the primary advantage a data lake offers by deferring this step?

options.label: A
options.text: It guarantees that all stored data is automatically cleaned and validated
options.rationale: Deferring schema definition does not clean or validate data; raw data lands as-is. Cleaning happens later in refinement zones, so this overstates what schema-on-read provides; the real benefit is immediate file storage.

options.label: B
options.text: It enforces referential integrity across all stored files
options.rationale: Referential integrity enforcement is a feature of structured relational databases, not file-first lakes. The lake's real advantage is the flexibility of immediate storage without a predefined structure.

options.label: C
options.text: It allows files to be stored immediately without first defining a rigid structure
options.rationale: Correct. A data lake stores files first and creates tables later, so data can be ingested immediately without the up-front rigid structure that schema-on-write demands.

options.label: D
options.text: It eliminates the need for any storage infrastructure
options.rationale: A data lake still relies on scalable object storage for files; it does not remove storage needs. Its real advantage is storing files first without predefining structure.

questions.stem: A data engineer must classify three inputs: rows in a MySQL table, a set of JSON and XML documents from a line-of-business app, and a stream of social media video clips. How should these be classified, in order?

options.label: A
options.text: Semi-structured, structured, unstructured
options.rationale: This misclassifies MySQL tables as semi-structured; relational tables with a fixed schema are structured, while JSON/XML are the semi-structured items. The correct order is structured, semi-structured, unstructured.

options.label: B
options.text: Structured, semi-structured, unstructured
options.rationale: MySQL tables are structured (fixed schema, relational), JSON/XML are semi-structured (some organization but not tabular), and video clips are unstructured (no predefined structure).

options.label: C
options.text: Structured, unstructured, semi-structured
options.rationale: This wrongly labels JSON/XML as unstructured and video as semi-structured; JSON/XML are semi-structured and video has no predefined structure, making it unstructured.

options.label: D
options.text: Unstructured, semi-structured, structured
options.rationale: This reverses the categories entirely; MySQL tables are structured (not unstructured) and video clips are unstructured (not structured).

questions.stem: At which institution was Apache Spark originally developed?

options.label: A
options.text: Google Research
options.rationale: Google is associated with the original MapReduce and GFS papers, not Spark. Spark originated at UC Berkeley's AMPLab.

options.label: B
options.text: UC Berkeley's AMPLab
options.rationale: Spark was originally developed at the AMPLab at the University of California, Berkeley.

options.label: C
options.text: The Apache Software Foundation from inception
options.rationale: Although Spark is now an Apache project, it did not originate at the Apache Software Foundation. It was first developed at UC Berkeley's AMPLab before later being donated to Apache.

options.label: D
options.text: Yahoo's Hadoop engineering team
options.rationale: Yahoo is closely tied to early Hadoop development, not Spark. Spark's origin is UC Berkeley's AMPLab.

questions.stem: In the MapReduce programming model, data is represented in a single consistent form as it flows from input through to final output. What is that form?

options.label: A
options.text: Fixed-width relational rows
options.rationale: MapReduce does not require relational rows; it processes arbitrary input. Throughout the model, data is represented as key-value pairs, not fixed-width rows.

options.label: B
options.text: Key-value (K,V) pairs
options.rationale: MapReduce represents data as key-value pairs throughout: Map emits (K,V), intermediate outputs are (K',V'), and Reduce emits (K'',V'').

options.label: C
options.text: JSON documents within collections
options.rationale: JSON documents in collections describe MongoDB's data model, not MapReduce. MapReduce operates on key-value pairs from end to end.

options.label: D
options.text: Fixed 128 MB binary blocks
options.rationale: 128 MB refers to an HDFS storage block size, not the logical data representation in MapReduce. The model represents data as key-value pairs throughout processing.

questions.stem: A bank needs to flag fraudulent transactions the instant they occur, so data must enter the platform immediately as it is generated rather than on a nightly schedule. Which ingestion approach and tool best fit this need?

options.label: A
options.text: Batch ingestion using Apache NiFi
options.rationale: Batch ingestion is scheduled at intervals (e.g., nightly) and would delay fraud detection; the immediate-as-generated need calls for real-time ingestion with Apache Kafka.

options.label: B
options.text: Real-time ingestion using Apache Kafka
options.rationale: Real-time ingestion processes data immediately as it is generated and is crucial for fraud detection and real-time analytics, with Apache Kafka as the example tool.

options.label: C
options.text: Batch ingestion using Apache Kafka
options.rationale: Kafka is associated with real-time, not batch; and batch's scheduled intervals would be too slow for instant fraud flagging, which requires real-time ingestion.

options.label: D
options.text: Real-time ingestion using Apache NiFi
options.rationale: NiFi is the example tool for batch ingestion; the correct real-time pairing for immediate fraud detection is real-time ingestion with Apache Kafka.

questions.stem: A team responds to growing workload by replacing their existing server with a single, much faster and more efficient machine rather than adding additional machines. Which scaling strategy does this represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Scale-out, because performance is being increased
options.rationale: Scale-out means adding more worker nodes to run in parallel, not replacing one machine with a faster one. Increasing performance can be done either way, so this label is wrong; replacing with a faster machine is scale-up.

options.label: B
options.text: Chunking, because the data is being split
options.rationale: Chunking is a storage-optimization technique for breaking data into pieces, not a hardware scaling strategy. No data was split here; the team upgraded one machine, which is scale-up.

options.label: C
options.text: Scale-up, because equipment is replaced with faster, more efficient hardware
options.rationale: Scale-up (vertical scaling) is precisely replacing equipment with faster or more efficient hardware on a single machine, which is what the team did.

options.label: D
options.text: Horizontal scaling, because more capacity is added
options.rationale: Horizontal scaling is synonymous with scale-out (adding nodes), the opposite of what was done. Upgrading a single machine to be faster is vertical scaling, i.e., scale-up.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes the role of analytical sandboxes within a big data architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Isolated environments where analysts perform discovery, exploratory data analysis, machine learning, and predictive modeling
options.rationale: Analytical sandboxes are isolated environments for discovery, ML, predictive modeling, and exploratory data analysis, matching this description exactly.

options.label: B
options.text: Distributed file systems that store raw data redundantly across many nodes for fault tolerance
options.rationale: That describes storage systems like HDFS or S3 in the storage layer, not sandboxes; sandboxes are isolated environments for analysis and experimentation.

options.label: C
options.text: Tools that import data immediately as it is generated for real-time analytics
options.rationale: That describes real-time ingestion; sandboxes are not ingestion tools but isolated environments for discovery and modeling.

options.label: D
options.text: Overarching components that enforce data access rules and protect against unauthorized use
options.rationale: That describes governance and security; sandboxes instead provide isolated spaces for analysts to explore data and build models.

questions.stem: Within the six pillars of modern data storage, which pillar is specifically aimed at access control, ownership, and quality checks that prevent a lake from degrading into a swamp?

options.label: A
options.text: Format Efficiency
options.rationale: Format Efficiency concerns using machine-optimized columnar formats like Parquet and ORC for analytical performance, not access control or quality governance to prevent a swamp.

options.label: B
options.text: Zone Architecture
options.rationale: Zone Architecture is about data maturing through Raw, Cleaned, and Curated layers, not about access control, ownership, and quality checks. Those belong to Strict Governance.

options.label: C
options.text: Files First
options.rationale: Files First addresses storing data initially as files in scalable buckets, not the access control, ownership, and quality enforcement that define Strict Governance.

options.label: D
options.text: Strict Governance
options.rationale: Correct. Strict Governance covers access control, ownership, and quality checks that prevent the lake from degrading into a swamp.

questions.stem: A developer constructs an RDD and then wants to change a few of its element values in place. Why is this not possible in Spark?

options.label: A
options.text: Because RDDs are immutable once constructed; their content cannot be modified
options.rationale: RDDs are immutable once constructed, so their content cannot be changed in place; instead, transformations produce new RDDs.

options.label: B
options.text: Because RDDs are stored only on disk and disk data is read-only
options.rationale: RDDs are stored in memory when possible (and on disk only as a fallback), and the real reason editing fails is immutability, not a read-only disk.

options.label: C
options.text: Because only the cluster manager, not the driver, is allowed to write to RDDs
options.rationale: The restriction is not about who may write; RDDs are immutable by design and cannot be modified by anyone after construction.

options.label: D
options.text: Because RDDs lack partitions, so individual elements cannot be located
options.rationale: RDDs are in fact split into partitions across nodes, so locating elements is possible; modification is impossible because RDDs are immutable.

questions.stem: An executor running part of a Spark job fails, and one partition of an RDD is lost. How does Spark recover that lost partition?

options.label: A
options.text: It restores the partition from a synchronous three-way disk replica maintained for every RDD
options.rationale: Spark does not depend on a mandatory three-way replica of every RDD; it instead recomputes lost data using lineage information that records how the RDD was constructed.

options.label: B
options.text: It uses the recorded lineage to recompute the lost partition from its inputs
options.rationale: Because Spark tracks lineage (how each RDD was constructed, as a DAG), it can efficiently recompute exactly the lost partition from its input data after an executor failure.

options.label: C
options.text: It aborts and restarts the entire job from the beginning on a new cluster
options.rationale: A full restart is unnecessary and inefficient. Lineage lets Spark recompute only the lost data rather than re-running everything.

options.label: D
options.text: It modifies the surviving RDD in place to fill in the missing values
options.rationale: RDDs are immutable and cannot be modified in place. Recovery works by recomputing the lost partition from lineage, not by editing existing data.

questions.stem: All of the following statements about Spark stages and the shuffle between them are true EXCEPT:

options.label: A
options.text: A job is divided into smaller sets of tasks called stages, where the output of one stage is the input of the next
options.rationale: This is true: each job is divided into stages, and one stage's output feeds the next, so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: A shuffle is always executed between two stages, regrouping or repartitioning data by a new grouping criterion
options.rationale: This is true: a shuffle always occurs between two stages, repartitioning data by a grouping criterion different from the previous stage, so it is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: The shuffle is a lightweight operation with negligible cost
options.rationale: This is the false statement and therefore the exception: the shuffle is described as a heavy operation, not a lightweight one with negligible cost.

options.label: D
options.text: A stage with no output edges stores its output in HDFS or a database
options.rationale: This is true: stages without output edges write their results to HDFS or a database, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: An engineer must choose a format for heavy analytical workloads on Spark and Amazon Athena, accepting that the files will not be directly human-readable. Which format is purpose-built for this analytical use?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON
options.rationale: JSON is semi-structured text good for web logs and nested APIs but produces massive file sizes and very slow analytical queries, unlike Parquet which is built for heavy analytics on Spark and Athena.

options.label: B
options.text: Parquet
options.rationale: Correct. Parquet is a columnar binary format ideal for heavy analytics with Spark and Amazon Athena; its trade-off is not being directly human-readable without processing.

options.label: C
options.text: CSV
options.rationale: CSV is row-based text with no schema and is highly inefficient for big-data workloads, the opposite of a format purpose-built for analytics like Parquet.

options.label: D
options.text: Avro
options.rationale: Avro is row-based binary suited to streaming and schema evolution, and is less ideal for deep analytical column queries, whereas Parquet is built precisely for those analytics.

questions.stem: A developer runs a Spark program on a single laptop to test it before deploying to a cluster. In local mode, what plays the role of the worker nodes?

options.label: A
options.text: Local threads act as 'pseudo-worker' nodes, parallelizing execution on the single PC
options.rationale: In local mode, local threads parallelize execution on a single PC and act as 'pseudo-worker' nodes, with a local scheduler running over the local file system, which is useful for development and testing.

options.label: B
options.text: Remote executors on a YARN cluster are still required
options.rationale: Local mode does not require a remote cluster. Local threads serve as pseudo-worker nodes on the single machine.

options.label: C
options.text: The driver program itself becomes unnecessary and is not started
options.rationale: The program still has a driver with a main method even in local mode. What stands in for worker nodes are local threads acting as pseudo-workers.

options.label: D
options.text: HDFS DataNodes running on the laptop emulate the cluster
options.rationale: Local mode runs over the local file system, not HDFS DataNodes. Local threads act as the pseudo-worker nodes.

questions.stem: All of the following statements about file formats are accurate EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: CSV and JSON are easy for humans to read but fail to perform well at big-data scale
options.rationale: This is true and therefore not the exception: CSV and JSON are human-readable text formats that are inefficient and slow at scale, so they fail at big-data workloads.

options.label: B
options.text: Parquet and ORC are compressed, schema-aware, and purpose-built for massive parallel processing
options.rationale: This is true and not the exception: Parquet and ORC are columnar binary formats that are compressed, schema-aware, and built for massive parallel analytics.

options.label: C
options.text: Avro is a columnar format optimized for deep analytical column queries
options.rationale: This is the exception because it is false: Avro is a row-based binary format suited to streaming and schema evolution, and it is explicitly less ideal for deep analytical column queries. The truly columnar analytical formats are Parquet and ORC.

options.label: D
options.text: ORC is most commonly associated with analytics in the Hadoop and Hive ecosystem
options.rationale: This is true and not the exception: ORC is good for Hadoop/Hive ecosystem analytics and is less common outside of it.

questions.stem: Comparing storage models, which statement correctly contrasts Hadoop MapReduce with Spark?

options.label: A
options.text: Hadoop MapReduce stores data only on disk, whereas Spark can store data in memory or on disk
options.rationale: MapReduce relies on disk-only storage between operations, while Spark can keep data in memory when possible and fall back to disk otherwise.

options.label: B
options.text: Both store data exclusively in memory
options.rationale: MapReduce is disk-only, so this is incorrect. Only Spark offers in-memory storage (with disk as a fallback).

options.label: C
options.text: Hadoop MapReduce stores data in memory, whereas Spark uses disk only
options.rationale: This reverses the truth. MapReduce is disk-only and Spark is the one that adds the in-memory option.

options.label: D
options.text: Neither system writes data to disk under any circumstances
options.rationale: MapReduce is fundamentally disk-based and even Spark spills to disk when memory is insufficient, so both can use disk. The correct contrast is disk-only versus in-memory-or-disk.

questions.stem: A team needs an engine that can serve batch jobs, interactive queries, and live streaming, and lets developers write code in Scala, Java, Python, or R. Given how Hadoop MapReduce and Spark differ in execution model and supported languages, which engine fits, and why?

options.label: A
options.text: Hadoop MapReduce, because it natively supports batch, interactive, and streaming execution
options.rationale: MapReduce's execution model is batch only, and its primary language is Java, so it does not meet these needs. Spark is the engine that supports batch, interactive, and streaming plus multiple languages.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark, because it supports batch, interactive, and streaming execution and offers Scala, Java, Python, and R
options.rationale: Spark's execution model spans batch, interactive, and streaming, and it provides programming environments in Scala, Java, Python, and R, exactly matching the requirements.

options.label: C
options.text: Hadoop MapReduce, because it offers Scala, Java, Python, and R while Spark is Java-only
options.rationale: This reverses the language support. MapReduce's primary environment is Java, while Spark is the one offering Scala, Java, Python, and R.

options.label: D
options.text: Neither, because both are limited to batch execution and Java
options.rationale: While that describes MapReduce, it is false for Spark, which adds interactive and streaming execution plus multiple languages. Spark therefore fits the requirement.

questions.stem: Which big data architecture layer functions as an overarching component that enforces rules and policies for data access, quality, and usability, with Apache Atlas serving as an example tool?

options.label: A
options.text: The governance, security, and monitoring layer
options.rationale: Governance enforces rules and policies for data access, quality, and usability, with Apache Atlas as the example, and this layer is overarching across the architecture.

options.label: B
options.text: The data ingestion layer
options.rationale: Ingestion imports and loads data; enforcing access, quality, and usability policies is the role of the governance, security, and monitoring layer.

options.label: C
options.text: The storage and processing layer
options.rationale: Storage and processing holds and transforms data; policy enforcement for access and usability belongs to governance, security, and monitoring.

options.label: D
options.text: The analytics and servicing layer
options.rationale: Analytics and servicing delivers insights and reporting; enforcing data policies and protection is the governance, security, and monitoring layer.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the stated advantages (pros) of NoSQL databases?

options.label: A
options.text: Big Data capability for massive volume, velocity, and variety
options.rationale: This IS a stated pro of NoSQL (handling Big Data's volume, velocity, and variety), so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: High availability via easy replication with no single point of failure
options.rationale: This IS a stated pro: NoSQL offers high availability through easy replication and no single point of failure, so it is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: A single standardized query language and API shared across all NoSQL databases
options.rationale: This is the EXCEPTION. NoSQL's listed CONS include the lack of standardization, where each database has its own API and learning curve. A single shared standard API is not a NoSQL advantage.

options.label: D
options.text: Agility from flexible schema design allowing changes without downtime
options.rationale: This IS a stated pro: NoSQL's flexible schema (agility) allows alteration without downtime, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: All of the following correctly describe column-family stores such as Cassandra, HBase, and Google BigTable EXCEPT:

options.label: A
options.text: They organize data by columns, grouping related columns into column families
options.rationale: This is TRUE of column-family stores: they organize data by columns and group related columns into column families, so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: A row need not have an entry for every column, making storage sparse
options.rationale: This is TRUE: column-family stores allow sparse rows where not every column has a value, so it is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: They deliver extreme performance on aggregation queries over a single column
options.rationale: This is TRUE: storing a column's values contiguously gives extreme performance on aggregations like SUM, COUNT, and AVG, so it is not the exception.

options.label: D
options.text: They excel at frequent updates to individual records and heavy transactional workloads
options.rationale: This is the EXCEPTION (the false statement). Column-family stores are poor at updating individual records and weak for heavy transactional workloads; their strength is columnar aggregation, not transactional updates.

questions.stem: A relational schema is 'explicit, schema-on-write' while a NoSQL store is 'dynamic, schema-on-read.' What is the practical difference this captures?

options.label: A
options.text: Schema-on-write validates structure when data is stored; schema-on-read interprets structure when data is retrieved
options.rationale: Correct. Schema-on-write enforces a predefined structure at insert time (rigid), while schema-on-read lets structure be interpreted at query/read time, allowing flexible, heterogeneous records.

options.label: B
options.text: Schema-on-write means data is never validated; schema-on-read means it is validated twice
options.rationale: Wrong. Schema-on-write enforces validation at write time, not never. The real distinction is when structure is applied: at write versus at read.

options.label: C
options.text: Schema-on-read requires a rigid predefined schema before any insert
options.rationale: Wrong. Requiring a rigid predefined schema before insert is schema-on-write (relational). Schema-on-read applies/interprets structure at read time, enabling flexibility.

options.label: D
options.text: Both approaches require altering all existing rows whenever a field is added
options.rationale: Wrong. Forcing changes across all existing rows is the rigid relational (schema-on-write) pain point. Schema-on-read tolerates new or missing fields without rewriting existing records.

questions.stem: An organization collects data that arrives too quickly, in too many formats, and at too large a scale for its conventional relational database systems to handle. According to the standard definition, what does this scenario describe?

options.label: A
options.text: Big data, which refers to data sets so large, fast, and complex that they cannot be processed with traditional data-processing systems
options.rationale: This matches the core definition: big data is collections of data sets so large, fast, and complex they exceed the capabilities of traditional databases and require powerful technologies and advanced algorithms.

options.label: B
options.text: A data warehouse, which is a centralized relational store optimized for structured reporting
options.rationale: A data warehouse is a traditional structured, centralized system; the scenario explicitly exceeds what such conventional systems can handle, which is the hallmark of big data rather than a warehouse.

options.label: C
options.text: A data mart, which is a small subject-specific subset of an enterprise database
options.rationale: A data mart is a small, focused slice of structured enterprise data; the scenario describes overwhelming volume, velocity, and variety that exceed traditional systems, which defines big data, not a small subset.

options.label: D
options.text: Metadata, which is descriptive information about other data
options.rationale: Metadata describes other data; it is not a category of overwhelming data sets. The scenario describes data exceeding traditional processing limits, which is the definition of big data.

questions.stem: An analyst has a dataset that will not fit in their workstation's RAM, but it does fit comfortably on the machine's local hard disk. According to the standard classification of dataset sizes, how is this dataset categorized?

options.label: A
options.text: Small, because it can be handled by a single computer
options.rationale: A small dataset is defined as one that fits comfortably in RAM (roughly 2-4 GB) so complex operations run without paging. The described dataset exceeds RAM, so it is not small; the correct category is medium.

options.label: B
options.text: Medium, because it exceeds RAM but fits on a single computer's persistent storage
options.rationale: By definition, a medium dataset cannot fit in RAM but does fit within a single computer's persistent (disk) storage, which exactly matches the scenario.

options.label: C
options.text: Large, because it cannot fit in RAM
options.rationale: A large dataset fits neither in RAM nor on a single computer's disk. Since this dataset still fits on local disk, it is medium, not large; not fitting in RAM alone does not make it large.

options.label: D
options.text: Large, because any disk-resident dataset requires a cluster
options.rationale: Disk residence does not force cluster use; a medium dataset is handled on one machine. Large is reserved for data that exceeds even a single disk, so this is medium.

questions.stem: The 'simple API' pillar of NoSQL is most often implemented with what kind of interface?

options.label: A
options.text: A complex standardized query language identical across all vendors
options.rationale: Wrong. The point of the simple API is to avoid a complex standard query language. NoSQL favors lightweight text-based protocols instead.

options.label: B
options.text: A binary-only proprietary protocol that forbids HTTP
options.rationale: Wrong. The simple API is typically text-based and HTTP-friendly, not a binary-only protocol forbidding HTTP. It commonly uses HTTP REST with JSON.

options.label: C
options.text: Text-based protocols, mostly HTTP REST with JSON, for low-level data manipulation
options.rationale: Correct. The simple API pillar relies on text-based protocols, most often HTTP REST with JSON, enabling low-level data manipulation without a complex query language.

options.label: D
options.text: A graphical drag-and-drop schema designer required for all queries
options.rationale: Wrong. A graphical schema designer is not what 'simple API' means. The pillar refers to lightweight text-based protocols such as HTTP REST with JSON.

questions.stem: How does the NameNode detect that a DataNode has become unavailable due to a crash or network partition?

options.label: A
options.text: By a shutdown message the DataNode sends before crashing
options.rationale: A crashed or partitioned node cannot reliably send a graceful shutdown message; detection relies instead on the missing heartbeat that the NameNode notices.

options.label: B
options.text: By the absence of a heartbeat from the DataNode
options.rationale: The NameNode detects an unavailable DataNode through the absence of its regular heartbeat, after which it marks that node and stops routing requests to it.

options.label: C
options.text: By the client reporting that its read failed
options.rationale: The NameNode does not depend on client failure reports to detect dead nodes; it monitors heartbeats and notices their absence to flag a DataNode as unavailable.

options.label: D
options.text: By a TaskTracker informing the JobTracker
options.rationale: TaskTracker/JobTracker communication concerns MapReduce task scheduling, not HDFS node liveness. The NameNode detects a dead DataNode via the absence of a heartbeat.

questions.stem: What is the primary responsibility of a DataNode in HDFS?

options.label: A
options.text: To store the cluster's complete metadata and file-to-block mapping
options.rationale: Storing all metadata and file-to-block mapping is the NameNode's job. DataNodes instead store the actual data blocks and serve them.

options.label: B
options.text: To accept client jobs and assign tasks to slaves
options.rationale: Accepting jobs and assigning tasks is the JobTracker's scheduling role, not a DataNode's. DataNodes simply store and serve data blocks.

options.label: C
options.text: To decide how files are split and read into key-value pairs
options.rationale: Deciding splitting/reading is handled by Input Format and Record Reader in MapReduce, not by DataNodes. A DataNode's role is to store and serve the physical data blocks.

options.label: D
options.text: To store the actual data blocks on local storage and serve them on request
options.rationale: DataNodes are the slave 'block servers' that store the actual data blocks in their local filesystem and store/retrieve them on request.

questions.stem: When running iterative jobs or multiple analyses over the same dataset, what was the core performance problem with Hadoop MapReduce that motivated Spark?

options.label: A
options.text: It could only run a single Map operator and no Reduce operator
options.rationale: MapReduce supports both Map and Reduce; the motivating problem was not a missing operator. The real issue was the heavy disk I/O caused by reading from and writing to HDFS each iteration or stage.

options.label: B
options.text: It required every job to be written exclusively in Python
options.rationale: Hadoop MapReduce's primary environment was Java, not Python, and language choice was not the core performance motivation. The motivation for Spark was reducing slow repeated disk I/O by keeping data in memory.

options.label: C
options.text: Each iteration or stage involved repeated, slow disk I/O reading from and writing to HDFS
options.rationale: Iterative jobs and repeated analyses in MapReduce write intermediate results to HDFS and read them back each pass; this disk I/O is very slow, which is exactly the bottleneck Spark addresses by keeping data in main memory.

options.label: D
options.text: It kept all intermediate data in main memory, exhausting RAM
options.rationale: This describes the in-memory approach Spark adopted, not MapReduce. MapReduce's problem was the opposite: it persisted intermediate data to disk, causing slow repeated I/O.

questions.stem: A team must reduce I/O on a multi-terabyte table whose analytical queries usually read only three of forty columns, and they want the format to dictate analytical performance. Which pillar of modern data storage most directly addresses this decision?

options.label: A
options.text: Active Catalogs, since a metadata layer reduces the columns scanned
options.rationale: Active Catalogs make data searchable and usable but do not reduce columns scanned. Reducing column I/O via columnar formats is the Format Efficiency pillar.

options.label: B
options.text: Format Efficiency, since machine-optimized columnar formats dictate analytical performance
options.rationale: Correct. Format Efficiency is the pillar stating that machine-optimized columnar formats like Parquet and ORC dictate analytical performance, which is exactly the lever for reading only a few of many columns.

options.label: C
options.text: Strict Governance, since access controls limit which columns are read
options.rationale: Governance controls access, ownership, and quality, not the I/O efficiency of reading few columns. The relevant lever is choosing a columnar format under Format Efficiency.

options.label: D
options.text: Files First, since storing data as files automatically minimizes I/O
options.rationale: Files First concerns storing data as files in scalable buckets, not the column-level read efficiency that columnar formats provide under Format Efficiency.

questions.stem: In 2004, a major technology company introduced a programming model for large-scale distributed processing on commodity hardware, which became foundational to the big data ecosystem. Which development was this?

options.label: A
options.text: The unveiling of Apache Hadoop
options.rationale: Hadoop was unveiled in 2006, after MapReduce. The 2004 distributed-processing programming model on commodity hardware was Google's MapReduce.

options.label: B
options.text: Google's introduction of MapReduce
options.rationale: In 2004 Google introduced MapReduce for large-scale distributed processing on commodity hardware, a foundational milestone in big data history.

options.label: C
options.text: The first appearance of the term 'Big Data'
options.rationale: The term 'Big Data' emerged in the 1990s, not 2004, and it is a term rather than a distributed-processing programming model. The 2004 milestone was MapReduce.

options.label: D
options.text: The rise of NoSQL and in-memory computing
options.rationale: NoSQL, in-memory, and cloud computing characterize the 2010-to-now era, not 2004. The 2004 commodity-hardware distributed model was MapReduce.

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the key takeaway about when columnar storage delivers the greatest benefit?

options.label: A
