options.text: When applications insert single rows one at a time in real time
options.rationale: Frequent single-row inserts favor row-based handling, not columnar. Columnar shines for analytical reads over many records targeting few columns, not for one-row-at-a-time writes.

options.label: B
options.text: When every query needs to read all columns of every record
options.rationale: If every query reads all columns, columnar offers little advantage because there is nothing irrelevant to skip. The benefit appears precisely when queries target only a fraction of columns.

options.label: C
options.text: When analytical queries on massive datasets target only a fraction of the available columns
options.rationale: Correct. Columnar storage is hyper-efficient for massive datasets where analytical queries touch only a small subset of columns, since it reads just those columns.

options.label: D
options.text: When the dataset is small and human readability is the priority
options.rationale: Small, human-readable needs favor text formats like CSV. Columnar binary formats target large-scale analytics, not small human-readable datasets.

questions.stem: Which Spark component is specifically designed to interact with structured datasets, supports the Hive Query Language and sources such as Parquet and JSON, and uses a query optimizer engine?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark Streaming
options.rationale: Spark Streaming processes live data streams in real time with RDD-like APIs; it is not the structured-query component. The structured-data component supporting Hive QL, Parquet, JSON, and a query optimizer is Spark SQL.

options.label: B
options.text: Spark SQL
options.rationale: Spark SQL interacts with structured data via SQL or querying APIs, is based on Datasets, supports the Hive Query Language and sources like Hive tables, Parquet, and JSON, and uses a query optimizer engine.

options.label: C
options.text: GraphFrames
options.rationale: GraphFrames is a graph library based on DataFrames and Python. It is not the general structured-query engine with Hive QL and a query optimizer; that is Spark SQL.

options.label: D
options.text: MLlib
options.rationale: MLlib is the machine-learning library offering parallel algorithms, not the SQL/structured-data engine. Querying structured datasets with Hive QL and a query optimizer is Spark SQL's role.

questions.stem: After the NameNode marks a DataNode as dead because of a missing heartbeat, what consequence may arise for the blocks that node was holding?

options.label: A
options.text: All affected files are permanently lost because their only copies are gone
options.rationale: HDFS keeps three replicas, so losing one DataNode does not destroy the only copy; other replicas remain. The actual consequence is that some blocks now have fewer copies and need re-replication.

options.label: B
options.text: Some blocks may fall below the desired replication level and require re-replication
options.rationale: Losing a DataNode can leave some blocks with fewer than three copies, so the system must re-replicate them to restore the required replication level.

options.label: C
options.text: The NameNode immediately reassigns its metadata duties to the dead node's replicas
options.rationale: Metadata duties belong solely to the NameNode (with a standby), not to DataNode replicas; data blocks do not take over metadata. The real effect is that under-replicated blocks need re-replication.

options.label: D
options.text: The cluster halts until the dead DataNode is physically replaced
options.rationale: Hadoop is designed to tolerate failures and keep running by redirecting work and re-replicating; it does not halt. The consequence is re-replication of now under-replicated blocks.

questions.stem: A development team must add a 'mobile photo upload' feature to an application backed by a traditional relational database with millions of existing rows. They discover the change could require hours of downtime. Which underlying property of relational databases is the primary cause of this problem?

options.label: A
options.text: The rigid, predefined schema must be altered and applied across all existing rows
options.rationale: Correct. Relational databases enforce a rigid, explicit schema (schema-on-write). Adding a new feature means altering that schema and propagating the change to millions of existing rows, which can require significant downtime.

options.label: B
options.text: Relational databases cannot store image data of any kind
options.rationale: Wrong. Relational databases can store binary/image data (e.g., as BLOBs). The downtime problem stems from altering the rigid schema across existing rows, not from an inability to store images.

options.label: C
options.text: The database automatically replicates every row to peer nodes during a schema change
options.rationale: Wrong. Peer-to-peer multi-master replication is a NoSQL trait, not the cause of RDBMS downtime. The relational downtime is caused by applying a rigid schema change to all existing rows.

options.label: D
options.text: Relational databases use schema-on-read, forcing a re-read of all rows
options.rationale: Wrong. Relational databases use schema-on-write, not schema-on-read (that is a NoSQL characteristic). The problem is the rigid predefined schema needing alteration across existing rows.

questions.stem: HDFS is described as being optimized for a particular access profile. Which characterization is accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Optimized for low-latency random access to many small files
options.rationale: HDFS is explicitly NOT optimized for low-latency access; that contradicts its design. It is optimized for high-throughput batch access to very large datasets.

options.label: B
options.text: Optimized for transactional updates with frequent in-place edits
options.rationale: HDFS follows a write-once, read-many model and is not built for frequent transactional in-place edits. Its optimization target is high-throughput batch reads.

options.label: C
options.text: Optimized for interactive sub-millisecond query responses
options.rationale: Sub-millisecond interactive responses describe low-latency systems, which HDFS is not. HDFS targets high-throughput batch processing of large datasets.

options.label: D
options.text: Optimized for high-throughput batch access to very large datasets
options.rationale: HDFS is designed for high-throughput batch access over very large (TB-PB) datasets on commodity clusters, rather than low-latency access.

questions.stem: A company is explaining why big data became feasible and necessary in recent years. Which factor is a recognized key driver of big data?

options.label: A
options.text: The decline of the public internet and digital content
options.rationale: The opposite is true: an explosion of digital content drives big data. A decline would reduce data, not drive its growth. A genuine driver is the growth of IoT and sensors.

options.label: B
options.text: The growth of the Internet of Things and connected sensors
options.rationale: The growth of IoT and sensors is an explicitly listed key driver of big data, generating vast volumes of data.

options.label: C
options.text: Rising costs of data storage and processing
options.rationale: A key driver is the cost reduction in storage and processing, not rising costs; cheaper storage and processing enabled big data.

options.label: D
options.text: A shift away from distributed computing toward single mainframes
options.rationale: Advances in distributed and cloud computing are a key driver; a shift back to single mainframes would contradict this enabler of big data.

questions.stem: What does the abbreviation RDD stand for, and what is its role in Spark?

options.label: A
options.text: Relational Data Document; a table-like structure for SQL queries
options.rationale: This is not the expansion or role of an RDD. RDD stands for Resilient Distributed Dataset and is Spark's primary abstraction, a distributed collection of objects.

options.label: B
options.text: Resilient Distributed Dataset; Spark's primary abstraction, a distributed collection of objects spread across cluster nodes
options.rationale: An RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset) is Spark's primary abstraction: a distributed collection of objects spread across the nodes of a cluster and split into partitions.

options.label: C
options.text: Redundant Disk Drive; a storage replication scheme
options.rationale: This is unrelated to Spark and confuses RDD with disk redundancy. RDD means Resilient Distributed Dataset, an in-memory distributed data abstraction.

options.label: D
options.text: Rapid Data Dispatcher; a scheduler that submits tasks
options.rationale: RDD is not a scheduler. It is the Resilient Distributed Dataset, the distributed collection of objects that is Spark's central abstraction.

questions.stem: A raw clickstream file lands at the path /data/raw/clickstream/2026/05/09/events.json, while a refined dataset sits at /data/curated/sales/year=2026/month=05/sales.parquet. Which conclusion is best supported by comparing these two paths?

options.label: A
options.text: The raw path holds untransformed JSON data, while the curated path holds partitioned, analytics-optimized Parquet data
options.rationale: Correct. The raw path stores untransformed JSON as received, while the curated path uses year/month partition folders and the columnar Parquet format optimized for analytics, reflecting progressive refinement.

options.label: B
options.text: Both paths use schema-on-write because the folder names define a fixed schema
options.rationale: Folder names express partitioning and zones, not a write-time enforced schema. A data lake applies schema-on-read over these files, so this is incorrect.

options.label: C
options.text: The curated Parquet file is human-readable while the raw JSON is binary
options.rationale: This is reversed: JSON is human-readable text, while Parquet is a columnar binary format not directly human-readable. The curated path is the binary, analytics-optimized one.

options.label: D
options.text: The raw path already applies partition pruning by date while the curated path does not
options.rationale: It is the curated path that uses explicit year= and month= partition folders enabling pruning; the raw path's date folders are not in the queried partitioned table form. This option reverses the situation.

questions.stem: Beyond faster queries, what is another direct benefit of partitioning that allows queries to scan less data?

options.label: A
options.text: It lowers cloud computing costs because less data is scanned
options.rationale: Correct. Less scanning means significantly faster queries and lower cloud computing costs, since cloud query engines often charge based on data scanned.

options.label: B
options.text: It automatically converts text files into columnar binary format
options.rationale: Partitioning organizes files into folders by filter columns; it does not change file formats. The cost benefit comes from scanning less data, not from format conversion.

options.label: C
options.text: It removes the need for any metadata catalog
options.rationale: Partitioning does not replace a data catalog; catalogs still map and describe datasets. Partitioning's added benefit is reduced scanning and therefore lower cost.

options.label: D
options.text: It guarantees ACID transactions on the underlying files
options.rationale: ACID guarantees come from lakehouse table formats, not from partitioning. Partitioning's payoff is reduced data scanned, yielding faster, cheaper queries.

questions.stem: In the CAP theorem, what does 'Partition tolerance' specifically guarantee?

options.label: A
options.text: Every node always returns the most current global value
options.rationale: Wrong. Always returning the most current global value describes Consistency. Partition tolerance is about continuing to operate when messages between nodes are dropped.

options.label: B
options.text: The database maintains operations despite network failures dropping messages between nodes
options.rationale: Correct. Partition tolerance means the database keeps operating even when network failures drop messages between nodes.

options.label: C
options.text: Every request always receives a response from the cluster
options.rationale: Wrong. Guaranteeing a response to every request is Availability. Partition tolerance concerns surviving network partitions that drop inter-node messages.

options.label: D
options.text: The data is partitioned evenly across all available disks
options.rationale: Wrong. Even disk partitioning is a storage layout concern. CAP's Partition tolerance is about surviving communication failures between nodes.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a Spark component as described in the Spark component stack?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark Streaming
options.rationale: Spark Streaming is a genuine component for processing live data streams in real time, so it belongs in the stack and is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: MLlib
options.rationale: MLlib is a real component: the machine-learning and data-mining library offering parallel algorithm implementations, so it belongs in the stack and is not the exception.

options.label: C
options.text: HBaseStore
options.rationale: There is no component called HBaseStore in Spark's stack. The described components are Spark Core, Spark SQL, Spark Streaming, MLlib, GraphX, and GraphFrames, making this the exception.

options.label: D
options.text: GraphX
options.rationale: GraphX is a real graph-processing library (subgraph searching, PageRank) within the stack, so it belongs and is not the exception.

questions.stem: Hadoop's design embraces the principle of moving computation to where the data resides rather than moving large datasets to the computation. What is this principle called, and why is it used?

options.label: A
options.text: Data locality, because moving computation is cheaper than moving large volumes of data
options.rationale: Data locality is the principle of moving computation to where data resides; it is used because transferring large data is far more expensive than sending the (small) computation to the data.

options.label: B
options.text: Write-once, read-many, because data is read repeatedly
options.rationale: Write-once read-many describes Hadoop's access pattern, not the placement of computation. Moving computation to data is the data locality principle, chosen to avoid costly large-data transfers.

options.label: C
options.text: Horizontal scalability, because it adds nodes to share work
options.rationale: Horizontal scalability is about scaling by adding nodes, a separate principle. The principle described, moving computation to the data, is data locality, used because moving computation is cheaper than moving large data.

options.label: D
options.text: Resource sharing, because nodes share the cluster
options.rationale: Resource sharing concerns multiple jobs sharing cluster resources, not where computation runs relative to data. The described concept is data locality, motivated by the cost of moving large data.

questions.stem: A team is migrating from a legacy system to a big data platform. The legacy system enforced its schema when data was written, while the new platform applies structure only when data is read. What are these two approaches called, respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: Schema-on-read and schema-on-write
options.rationale: The labels are reversed. Enforcing schema at write time is schema-on-write (traditional), and applying structure at read time is schema-on-read (big data).

options.label: B
options.text: Schema-on-write and schema-on-read
options.rationale: Traditional systems use a predefined, fixed schema applied at write time (schema-on-write), whereas big data platforms apply a flexible schema at read time (schema-on-read).

options.label: C
options.text: Vertical scaling and horizontal scaling
options.rationale: These terms describe scaling strategy (scale-up vs scale-out), not when schema is applied; the question is about schema-on-write versus schema-on-read.

options.label: D
options.text: Batch processing and stream processing
options.rationale: Batch versus stream describes processing timing, not schema enforcement. Applying schema at write versus read is schema-on-write versus schema-on-read.

questions.stem: All of the following correctly pair an ecosystem tool with its primary purpose EXCEPT which one?

options.label: A
options.text: Sqoop transfers data between relational databases and Hadoop (import to HDFS, export back)
options.rationale: This pairing is correct: Sqoop ('SQL-to-Hadoop') integrates data by importing from and exporting to RDBMS, so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: Mahout coordinates distributed processes by providing synchronization, naming, and configuration services
options.rationale: This is the incorrect pairing and thus the exception. Mahout provides scalable machine-learning algorithms on Hadoop; the synchronization/naming/configuration coordination role belongs to ZooKeeper.

options.label: C
options.text: Flume reliably collects, aggregates, and moves large amounts of log data into HDFS
options.rationale: This pairing is correct: Flume is the reliable distributed system for ingesting large log data into HDFS, so it is not the exception.

options.label: D
options.text: Hive provides SQL-like access to HDFS via HQL, converting queries into MapReduce
options.rationale: This pairing is correct: Hive offers SQL-like (HQL) access to HDFS and converts queries to MapReduce, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: In MapReduce internals, what is the purpose of the partitioner, and why must the map nodes agree on it?

options.label: A
options.text: It determines which reduce task a given (K,V) pair is sent to, and map nodes must agree so each key consistently lands in the same partition
options.rationale: The partitioner assigns each (K,V) to a specific reduce task/partition; all map nodes must use the same scheme so that every occurrence of a given key is routed to the same reducer.

options.label: B
options.text: It defines how input files are split into units of work for map tasks
options.rationale: Defining how files are split is the Input Format / input split concept, not the partitioner. The partitioner instead routes intermediate (K,V) pairs to the correct reduce task.

options.label: C
options.text: It writes the reducers' output pairs to the final output files
options.rationale: Writing final output is the Output Format's role. The partitioner operates on the map side to assign intermediate pairs to reduce partitions.

options.label: D
options.text: It sorts the intermediate keys within each partition before reduce
options.rationale: Sorting within a partition is part of the merge/shuffle-and-sort step, not the partitioner's job. The partitioner only decides the destination partition for each (K,V) pair.

questions.stem: A team chooses a NoSQL store that follows the BASE model instead of strict ACID. Which guarantee are they primarily trading away in exchange for higher availability and scalability?

options.label: A
options.text: The ability to store any data at all during peak load
options.rationale: Wrong. BASE systems remain 'Basically Available' and can still store data under load. What is relaxed is strict, instantaneous consistency, not storage capability.

options.label: B
options.text: The ability to run on commodity hardware
options.rationale: Wrong. BASE systems are designed to run on distributed commodity hardware. They give up strict immediate consistency, not commodity-hardware support.

options.label: C
options.text: Strict, instantaneous consistency in favor of eventual consistency
options.rationale: Correct. BASE relaxes the strict, instantaneous consistency of ACID toward eventual consistency, gaining availability and scalability in distributed deployments.

options.label: D
options.text: The ability to query data by key
options.rationale: Wrong. Querying by key is unrelated to the ACID/BASE trade-off. The property given up under BASE is strict immediate consistency, replaced by eventual consistency.

questions.stem: Consider the classic MapReduce word-count job. After the map tasks have each read their input split and emitted a <word, 1> pair for every word, what does the reduce phase do?

options.label: A
options.text: It re-reads the original input files to recount each word independently
options.rationale: Reduce never re-reads the raw input; it consumes the grouped intermediate output from the maps. Its job is to combine the per-word counts that the maps already emitted.

options.label: B
options.text: It splits the input file into blocks for the map tasks
options.rationale: Splitting input into blocks/splits happens before mapping, not in reduce. Reduce instead aggregates the grouped intermediate counts into final totals.

options.label: C
options.text: It assigns each (K,V) pair to a partition for the appropriate reducer
options.rationale: Assigning pairs to partitions is the partitioner's job during the map side, not reduce's work. Reduce combines the grouped per-word counts into final totals.

options.label: D
options.text: It combines the grouped <word, 1> values for each key into a final per-word total
options.rationale: In word count, after same keys are grouped, the reduce function sums the 1s for each word to produce the final total count per word.

questions.stem: An organization wants infinitely scalable storage with automated security and native analytics integration, without managing physical clusters. Which option is described as the modern standard foundation for data lakes?

options.label: A
options.text: HDFS deployed on owned hardware
options.rationale: HDFS on owned hardware is the legacy approach that requires managing physical on-premise machines, contradicting the goal of avoiding cluster management; the modern standard is Amazon S3.

options.label: B
options.text: A single relational SQL server
options.rationale: A relational SQL server uses rigid schema-on-write tables and does not offer infinitely scalable object storage, so it is not the modern lake foundation that Amazon S3 provides.

options.label: C
options.text: A local network-attached drive array
options.rationale: A local drive array is physical infrastructure the organization must manage and does not provide cloud-scale elasticity or native analytics integration like Amazon S3 does.

options.label: D
options.text: Amazon S3
options.rationale: Correct. Amazon S3 is described as the modern standard and common foundation: infinitely scalable, with automated security and native analytics integration, requiring no physical cluster management.

questions.stem: Which of the following data sources is the clearest example of unstructured data?

options.label: A
options.text: A table of customer records stored in an Oracle relational database
options.rationale: Relational database tables follow a fixed schema and are organized, making them structured data, not unstructured.

options.label: B
options.text: An XML feed exported from a line-of-business application
options.rationale: XML has some organization through tags but is not tabular, so it is semi-structured rather than unstructured.

options.label: C
options.text: Video and audio recordings posted to a social platform
options.rationale: Video, audio, images, and social posts have no predefined structure, which is the definition of unstructured data.

options.label: D
options.text: A JSON document returned by a web service
options.rationale: JSON carries organization through keys and values but is not tabular, classifying it as semi-structured, not unstructured.

questions.stem: When are RDD partitions stored on the local disk of an executor rather than in main memory?

options.label: A
options.text: Always, because RDDs are a disk-only abstraction
options.rationale: RDDs are kept in main memory whenever possible; they are not disk-only. Disk is used only as a fallback when memory is insufficient.

options.label: B
options.text: Only when the driver explicitly requests disk persistence for fault tolerance
options.rationale: The fallback to disk is driven by insufficient memory, not by a mandatory driver request for fault tolerance. Spark prefers main memory and spills to local disk when memory runs short.

options.label: C
options.text: When there is not enough memory to hold them, in which case they spill to local disk
options.rationale: RDDs are stored in the executors' main memory when possible, but if there is not enough memory they are placed on local disk instead.

options.label: D
options.text: Never, because executors have no access to disk
options.rationale: Executors can and do use local disk as a fallback. RDDs go to local disk specifically when memory is insufficient.

questions.stem: Within the Spark component stack, which component provides the basic functionality, including task scheduling, memory management, and fault recovery, and the APIs to create RDDs?

options.label: A
options.text: Spark SQL
options.rationale: Spark SQL is built on top of Spark Core to query structured data via SQL. The foundational functionality (task scheduling, memory management, fault recovery, RDD APIs) is provided by Spark Core.

options.label: B
options.text: MLlib
options.rationale: MLlib provides parallel machine-learning algorithms and sits on top of Spark Core. The basic scheduling, memory management, and RDD APIs come from Spark Core itself.

options.label: C
options.text: Spark Core
options.rationale: Spark Core supplies the basic functionality used by all components: task scheduling, memory management, and fault recovery, and exposes the APIs to create RDDs and apply transformations and actions.

options.label: D
options.text: GraphX
options.rationale: GraphX is a graph-processing library layered on Spark Core. The core scheduling, memory, fault-recovery, and RDD-creation functionality belongs to Spark Core.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a stated characteristic of HDFS?

options.label: A
options.text: Scalable: nodes can be added without changing data formats or jobs
options.rationale: This IS a characteristic of HDFS: it scales by adding nodes without requiring changes to formats or jobs, so it is not the exception.

options.label: B
options.text: Cost-effective: runs on commodity servers at low cost per terabyte
options.rationale: This IS a characteristic of HDFS: it uses commodity servers giving a low cost per terabyte, so it belongs in the list.

options.label: C
options.text: Low-latency: guarantees sub-millisecond random reads and writes
options.rationale: This is the exception. HDFS is optimized for high-throughput batch access and is explicitly NOT a low-latency system, so guaranteeing sub-millisecond access is not a characteristic of HDFS.

options.label: D
options.text: Fault tolerant: redirects work to another data location when a node is lost
options.rationale: This IS a characteristic of HDFS: on node loss it redirects work to another data location, providing fault tolerance, so it is not the exception.

questions.stem: A telecom firm streams readings from RFID tags, smart meters, and sensors that must be processed almost as soon as they are generated. Which of the 5 Vs of big data does this requirement most directly represent?

options.label: A
options.text: Volume
options.rationale: Volume refers to the massive size of the data accumulated over time; the scenario emphasizes the speed of generation and near-real-time processing, which is Velocity.

options.label: B
options.text: Velocity
options.rationale: Velocity is the high-speed generation and near-real-time processing of data, exemplified by RFID, sensors, and smart metering, which is exactly what the scenario describes.

options.label: C
options.text: Veracity
options.rationale: Veracity concerns data quality, noise, bias, and reliability, not the speed of arrival. The defining feature here is rapid generation and processing, which is Velocity.

options.label: D
options.text: Variety
options.rationale: Variety refers to the many different formats and sources of data; the scenario stresses how fast the readings must be handled, which corresponds to Velocity.

questions.stem: According to the CAP theorem, why must a distributed database make a trade-off between Consistency and Availability in practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Because consistency and availability are mathematically the same property
options.rationale: Wrong. They are distinct properties (identical view vs. always responding). The trade-off arises because partitions are unavoidable, forcing a choice between C and A.

options.label: B
options.text: Because partition tolerance is optional and rarely needed
options.rationale: Wrong. In real distributed systems partitions are inevitable, so partition tolerance is effectively required, which forces the C-versus-A trade-off.

options.label: C
options.text: Because network partitions are inevitable, so when one occurs the system must choose to favor C or A
options.rationale: Correct. Since network partitions are unavoidable in distributed systems, partition tolerance is a given, and during a partition the system must favor either Consistency or Availability.

options.label: D
options.text: Because a system can fully guarantee all three properties at once
options.rationale: Wrong. CAP states you can fully guarantee only two of the three. With partitions unavoidable, the practical choice reduces to C versus A.

questions.stem: A MapReduce job is running, and one task is progressing far more slowly than all the others, threatening to dominate the total job time. According to MapReduce's design, how is this straggler handled?

options.label: A
options.text: The job is aborted and restarted from the beginning
options.rationale: MapReduce does not restart whole jobs over a slow task. It launches speculative copies of the straggler so the faster copy can finish first.

options.label: B
options.text: The slow task is skipped and its data is dropped from the result
options.rationale: Dropping a task's data would produce incorrect results, which MapReduce avoids. Instead it runs a duplicate (speculative) copy of the straggler to complete the work faster.

options.label: C
options.text: A speculative duplicate copy of the slow task is launched, and the first to finish commits
options.rationale: Speculative execution locates slow stragglers and runs replicated copies that optimistically commit before the straggler, since a job is dominated by its slowest task.

options.label: D
options.text: The JobTracker assumes the TaskTracker has crashed after one minute and re-executes all its tasks
options.rationale: That one-minute crash-detection mechanism handles a TaskTracker that stops communicating (failure), not a task that is merely slow. A slow-but-alive task is handled by speculative execution of a duplicate copy.

questions.stem: When contrasting traditional data systems with big data systems, which pairing of scaling strategies is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Traditional systems scale out across clusters; big data systems scale up on a single powerful server
options.rationale: This is reversed. Traditional systems typically scale up (vertical) on more powerful single machines, while big data systems scale out (horizontal) across clusters.

options.label: B
options.text: Both traditional and big data systems rely primarily on vertical scaling
options.rationale: Big data systems emphasize horizontal scaling across distributed clusters; only traditional systems lean on vertical scaling, so this is incorrect.

options.label: C
options.text: Traditional systems use vertical scaling (scale-up); big data systems use horizontal scaling (scale-out)
options.rationale: Traditional systems scale up by adding power to a single machine, while big data systems scale out by adding more commodity nodes to a distributed cluster, exactly as described.

options.label: D
options.text: Both traditional and big data systems rely primarily on horizontal scaling
options.rationale: Traditional systems characteristically use vertical scaling on monolithic hardware; horizontal scaling is the distinguishing big data approach, so claiming both use it is wrong.

questions.stem: Within the CAP theorem, how is 'Consistency' defined?

options.label: A
options.text: Every request receives a response so the system stays operational
options.rationale: Wrong. 'Every request receives a response' defines Availability, not Consistency. Consistency means every user sees an identical view of the data at any instant.

options.label: B
options.text: The system keeps operating despite dropped messages between nodes
options.rationale: Wrong. Operating despite dropped messages is Partition tolerance. Consistency is about all users having an identical, linearizable view of the data.

options.label: C
options.text: Every user has an identical view of the data at any given instant
options.rationale: Correct. In CAP, Consistency (linearizability) means every user has an identical view of the data at any given instant.

options.label: D
options.text: Data is automatically replicated to at least three nodes
options.rationale: Wrong. A replica count is an implementation detail, not the CAP definition. Consistency means all users observe an identical view at any instant.

questions.stem: A traditional relational database such as MySQL or PostgreSQL is typically placed on which edge of the CAP triangle, and under what condition does that placement hold?

options.label: A
options.text: AP edge, working only when strong consistency is disabled
options.rationale: Wrong. Traditional RDBMS sit on the CA edge, not AP. AP databases (e.g., Cassandra) favor availability under partitions, which is the opposite priority.

options.label: B
options.text: CP edge, shutting down out-of-sync nodes during partitions
options.rationale: Wrong. Shutting down out-of-sync nodes during partitions describes CP systems like MongoDB or HBase. Classic RDBMS are CA and assume no partitions.

options.label: C
options.text: CA edge, holding only when there are no physical network partitions
options.rationale: Correct. Traditional RDBMS occupy the CA edge (Consistency + Availability) and work as such only when there are no physical network partitions, i.e., a single node or tightly coupled system.

options.label: D
options.text: CA edge, holding precisely because it tolerates frequent partitions
options.rationale: Wrong. The CA placement holds only when partitions do NOT occur. A CA system does not provide partition tolerance, so it cannot rely on tolerating frequent partitions.

questions.stem: A scheduled job moves the day's records into the data platform every night at midnight rather than continuously. Which type of ingestion does this describe?

options.label: A
options.text: Real-time ingestion
options.rationale: Real-time ingestion processes data immediately as it is generated; a nightly scheduled job is interval-based, which is batch ingestion.

options.label: B
options.text: Batch ingestion
options.rationale: Batch ingestion is scheduled or interval-based, such as a nightly load, exactly matching a job that runs every midnight.

options.label: C
options.text: Continuous stream ingestion
options.rationale: Stream ingestion continuously handles data as it flows in; a once-per-night scheduled load is interval-based and therefore batch ingestion, not continuous.

options.label: D
options.text: Schema-on-read ingestion
options.rationale: Schema-on-read describes when structure is applied to data, not the timing of loading. A nightly scheduled load is batch ingestion.

questions.stem: Which of the following columns is the WORST choice to partition a data lake table by, because it would create too many tiny folders?

options.label: A
options.text: region
options.rationale: Region is a recommended partition column because it has relatively few distinct values and is frequently filtered, producing manageable folder counts. The poor choice is a unique identifier like transaction_id.

options.label: B
options.text: department
options.rationale: Department is a good partition column with a small set of values that is commonly filtered, unlike a unique identifier which fragments storage into countless tiny folders.

options.label: C
options.text: event_type
options.rationale: Event_type is a recommended partition column with limited distinct values used in filters. The damaging choice is a unique-per-row identifier like transaction_id.

options.label: D
options.text: transaction_id
options.rationale: Correct. Partitioning by a unique identifier such as transaction_id creates an enormous number of tiny folders, which is explicitly advised against.

questions.stem: During preprocessing, a team removes and corrects inaccurate records, adds external context to enrich entries, converts values into a standardized format, and breaks unstructured text into structured fields. Which transformation specifically refers to converting data into a standardized format?

options.label: A
options.text: Data cleaning
options.rationale: Data cleaning removes or corrects inaccurate records; converting data into a standardized format is normalization, not cleaning.

options.label: B
options.text: Data enrichment
options.rationale: Enrichment adds extra information or context to data; standardizing the format is normalization, a different transformation.

options.label: C
options.text: Normalization
options.rationale: Normalization is the transformation that converts data into a standardized format, exactly as described.

options.label: D
options.text: Structuring
options.rationale: Structuring breaks unstructured or semi-structured data into structured form; producing a standardized format is normalization.

questions.stem: Which storage technology represents the LEGACY approach of dividing files into hardware blocks distributed across physical on-premise machines?

options.label: A
options.text: Amazon S3 cloud object storage
options.rationale: Amazon S3 is the modern cloud object storage standard, storing data as objects in scalable buckets, not the legacy on-premise block-distribution approach that HDFS represents.

options.label: B
options.text: A distributed file system such as HDFS
options.rationale: Correct. HDFS is the legacy distributed file system that divides files into hardware blocks distributed across physical on-premise machines.

options.label: C
options.text: Azure Data Lake cloud buckets
options.rationale: Azure Data Lake is a modern cloud object storage service, not the legacy on-premise block-based file system that HDFS represents.

options.label: D
options.text: Google Cloud Storage buckets
options.rationale: Google Cloud Storage is modern cloud object storage based on buckets and objects, contrasting with the legacy hardware-block distribution of HDFS.

questions.stem: Big Data workloads strain traditional relational systems because of the 'scale-up wall.' What scaling limitation does this describe?

options.label: A
options.text: RDBMS depends on horizontal scaling, which is too slow for Big Data
options.rationale: Wrong. Horizontal scaling (scale-out) is the NoSQL approach, not the RDBMS limitation. The scale-up wall refers to RDBMS relying on vertical scaling.

options.label: B
options.text: RDBMS relies on vertical scaling (a bigger single server), which has physical and cost limits
options.rationale: Correct. Relational systems traditionally scale vertically by buying a bigger server, while Big Data needs horizontal scaling across many cheaper machines. The ceiling on a single server is the 'scale-up wall.'

options.label: C
options.text: RDBMS can only ever run on a single CPU core
options.rationale: Wrong. Relational databases use multiple cores; the limitation is the reliance on a single bigger machine (vertical scaling) rather than distributing across many servers.

options.label: D
options.text: RDBMS cannot enforce ACID transactions beyond a fixed data size
options.rationale: Wrong. ACID enforcement is not the scaling bottleneck described here. The scale-up wall is about vertical scaling reaching its physical/cost limits, not a hard data-size cap on ACID.

questions.stem: The 'distributed and shared-nothing' pillar of NoSQL is associated with which combination of traits?

options.label: A
options.text: Auto-scaling, peer-to-peer multi-master replication, and eventual consistency
options.rationale: Correct. The shared-nothing pillar provides auto-scaling and peer-to-peer multi-master replication, requiring less coordination and yielding eventual consistency.

options.label: B
options.text: A single master node that all writes must pass through for strong consistency
options.rationale: Wrong. A single master enforcing strong consistency contradicts shared-nothing design, which uses peer-to-peer multi-master replication and eventual consistency.

options.label: C
options.text: Vertical scaling on one large shared-memory machine
options.rationale: Wrong. Vertical scaling on shared memory is the RDBMS scale-up model. Shared-nothing means distributing across independent nodes with auto-scaling.

options.label: D
options.text: Centralized coordination of every node before any write completes
options.rationale: Wrong. Shared-nothing aims for less coordination, not centralized coordination of every write. That heavy coordination would undermine its high distribution and availability.

questions.stem: An executive lists the benefits her company gained from adopting big data. Which of the following is a recognized advantage of big data?

options.label: A
options.text: Guaranteed elimination of all algorithmic bias
options.rationale: Algorithmic bias is listed as an ethical challenge of big data, not an advantage; big data does not guarantee its elimination. A genuine advantage is enhanced decision-making.

options.label: B
options.text: Automatic compliance with all data privacy regulations
options.rationale: Compliance with regulations like GDPR is a security and privacy challenge, not an automatic advantage; an actual advantage is enhanced decision-making.

options.label: C
options.text: Removal of the need for data cleaning and quality control
options.rationale: Data quality and cleaning remain a recognized data challenge; big data does not remove that need. A genuine advantage is enhanced decision-making.

options.label: D
options.text: Enhanced decision-making through deeper insights
options.rationale: Enhanced decision-making is an explicitly listed advantage of big data, alongside efficiency, customer insights, and cost-effectiveness.

questions.stem: A consulting report groups big data challenges into categories. Which challenge is correctly matched to its category?

options.label: A
options.text: Algorithmic bias is a technical challenge
options.rationale: Algorithmic bias is classified as an ethical challenge, alongside data ownership and responsible AI, not a technical one.

options.label: B
options.text: Scalability is a security and privacy challenge
options.rationale: Scalability is a technical challenge (with storage management, real-time processing, and reliability), not a security and privacy challenge.

options.label: C
options.text: Compliance with GDPR is a security and privacy challenge
options.rationale: Compliance, including GDPR, sits under security and privacy challenges, together with data breaches and encryption, matching this category correctly.

options.label: D
options.text: Integrating data from multiple sources is an ethical challenge
options.rationale: Integration from multiple sources is a data challenge, not an ethical one; ethical challenges include bias, ownership, and responsible AI.
